title: Find the Different Ones!
description: You are given an array $$$a$$$ of $$$n$$$ integers, and $$$q$$$ queries. Each query is represented by two integers $$$l$$$ and $$$r$$$ ($$$1 \le l \le r \le n$$$). Your task is to find, for each query, two indices $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$ (or determine that they do not exist) such that: $$$l \le i \le r$$$; $$$l \le j \le r$$$; $$$a_i \ne a_j$$$. In other words, for each query, you need to find a pair of different elements among $$$a_l, a_{l+1}, \dots, a_r$$$, or report that such a pair does not exist.
input_spec: The first line of the input contains a single integer $$$t$$$ ($$$1 \le t \le 10^4$$$) — the number of test cases. The descriptions of the test cases follow. The first line of each test case contains a single integer $$$n$$$ ($$$2 \le n \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$) — the length of the array $$$a$$$. The second line of each test case contains $$$n$$$ integers $$$a_1, a_2, \dots, a_n$$$ ($$$1 \le a_i \le 10^6$$$) — the elements of the array $$$a$$$. The third line of each test case contains a single integer $$$q$$$ ($$$1 \le q \le 2 \cdot 10^5$$$) — the number of queries. The next $$$q$$$ lines contain two integers each, $$$l$$$ and $$$r$$$ ($$$1 \le l  \lt  r \le n$$$) — the boundaries of the query. It is guaranteed that the sum of the values of $$$n$$$ across all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \cdot 10^5$$$. Similarly, it is guaranteed that the sum of the values of $$$q$$$ across all test cases does not exceed $$$2 \cdot 10^5$$$.
output_spec: For each query, output two integers separated by space: $$$i$$$ and $$$j$$$ ($$$l \le i, j \le r$$$), for which $$$a_i \ne a_j$$$. If such a pair does not exist, output $$$i=-1$$$ and $$$j=-1$$$. You may separate the outputs for the test cases with empty lines. This is not a mandatory requirement.

routes.name: Precompute nearest transition, answer in O(1)
routes.description: For each index i, precompute the position of the nearest earlier adjacent-pair transition (where a[j] != a[j-1] for some j <= i). Then for each query (l, r), check whether such a transition exists inside the range. This gives O(n) preprocessing and O(1) per query.

observations.title: A range has no valid pair if and only if all its elements are equal
observations.description: The query asks for any two indices i, j in [l, r] with a[i] != a[j]. This is impossible only when every element in the range has the same value. Equivalently, a valid pair exists if and only if there is at least one position k in [l+1, r] where a[k] != a[k-1].

hints.nudge: When is it impossible to find two different elements in a subarray?
hints.question: If every consecutive pair in a[l..r] satisfies a[k] = a[k-1], what can you conclude about all elements in the range?
hints.formal: All elements in a[l..r] are equal iff a[k] = a[k-1] for every k in [l+1, r]. Thus a valid pair (i, j) with a[i] != a[j] exists iff there is some k in [l+1, r] where a[k] != a[k-1]. That adjacent pair (k-1, k) is itself a valid answer.

observations.title: Finding one adjacent difference in the range is sufficient
observations.description: You do not need to find an arbitrary pair -- any position k where a[k] != a[k-1] within [l, r] directly gives the answer indices k-1 and k. So the problem reduces to: for a query range [l, r], does there exist any adjacent transition inside it?

hints.nudge: Do you need to find the optimal or most separated pair, or will any pair of different elements work?
hints.question: If you know a specific position k in [l+1, r] where a[k] != a[k-1], what pair of indices would you output?
hints.formal: Any position k with l < k <= r and a[k] != a[k-1] gives a valid output: the pair (k-1, k). This is the simplest witness of a non-constant range.

observations.title: Precompute the nearest transition at or before each index
observations.description: Define p[i] as the largest index j <= i such that a[j] != a[j-1] (or -1 if no such j exists for j <= i). Build p in a single left-to-right pass: if a[i] != a[i-1] then p[i] = i, otherwise p[i] = p[i-1]. This array lets you quickly find the rightmost transition in any prefix.

hints.nudge: Can you precompute something for each index that tells you where the most recent change happened?
hints.question: If p[i-1] stores the latest transition up to index i-1, and you now look at position i, how do you compute p[i]?
hints.formal: Set p[0] = -1 (no transition possible at the first element). For i from 1 to n-1: if a[i] != a[i-1] then p[i] = i, else p[i] = p[i-1]. After this O(n) scan, p[i] is the largest j <= i where a[j] != a[j-1], or -1 if no transition exists in a[0..i].

observations.title: Answer each query in O(1) using the precomputed array
observations.description: For query (l, r), look up p[r] (the rightmost transition at or before index r). If p[r] >= l+1 (using 0-indexed: the transition is inside the query range), output the pair (p[r]-1, p[r]). Otherwise, no transition exists in [l, r] and the answer is -1 -1. Each query is answered in O(1).

hints.nudge: You have the latest transition at or before r. How do you check whether it falls inside the query range?
hints.question: If p[r] < l (the latest transition before r is entirely before the query starts), what does that tell you about the subarray a[l..r]?
hints.formal: Convert to 0-indexed: l' = l-1, r' = r-1. If p[r'] >= l' + 1 (there is a transition a[p[r']] != a[p[r']-1] with both indices in [l', r']), output (p[r']-1+1, p[r']+1) in 1-indexed form. Otherwise output -1 -1. Total complexity: O(n + q) per test case.

routes.name: Binary search on stored transition positions
routes.description: Collect all positions where adjacent elements differ into a sorted list. For each query (l, r), binary search for the first transition position in [l+1, r]. If one exists, output the adjacent pair; otherwise output -1 -1.

observations.title: A range has no valid pair if and only if all its elements are equal
observations.description: The query asks for any two indices i, j in [l, r] with a[i] != a[j]. This is impossible only when every element in the range has the same value. Equivalently, a valid pair exists if and only if there is at least one adjacent transition a[k] != a[k-1] for some k in [l+1, r].

hints.nudge: When is it impossible to find two different elements in a subarray?
hints.question: If every consecutive pair in a[l..r] satisfies a[k] = a[k-1], what can you conclude about all elements in the range?
hints.formal: All elements in a[l..r] are equal iff a[k] = a[k-1] for every k in [l+1, r]. A valid pair exists iff there is some k in [l+1, r] where a[k] != a[k-1].

observations.title: Collect all adjacent transition positions into a sorted list
observations.description: Scan the array once and record every index k (1-indexed, from 2 to n) where a[k] != a[k-1]. Store these in a list. Since you scan left to right, the list is already sorted in increasing order.

hints.nudge: What if you wrote down every position where the array value changes compared to the previous position?
hints.question: If you iterate from index 2 to n and record i whenever a[i] != a[i-1], what property does the resulting list have?
hints.formal: Build list T = [k for k in range(2, n+1) if a[k] != a[k-1]]. T is sorted since we iterate left to right. T has at most n-1 entries.

observations.title: Binary search for a transition inside the query range
observations.description: For a query (l, r), you need any k in T with l+1 <= k <= r. Use binary search (lower_bound) to find the first element in T that is >= l+1. If that element exists and is <= r, you have found a transition inside the range.

hints.nudge: You have a sorted list of transition positions. How do you quickly check if any of them fall in a given interval?
hints.question: What standard algorithm finds the smallest element in a sorted array that is at least some value? What do you check after finding it?
hints.formal: Use lower_bound on T for value l+1. Let it point to T[idx]. If idx < |T| and T[idx] <= r, then k = T[idx] is a transition in [l+1, r], and (k-1, k) is a valid pair. Otherwise output -1 -1. Each query takes O(log n).

observations.title: Output the adjacent pair at the found transition
observations.description: Once you find a transition position k (where a[k] != a[k-1]) inside the query range, output the pair (k-1, k). Both indices are within [l, r] and their values differ, satisfying the query. The total complexity is O(n + q log n) per test case.

hints.nudge: You found a position k where a[k] != a[k-1]. What two indices do you output?
hints.question: Is k-1 guaranteed to be >= l? Why?
hints.formal: Since k >= l+1 (from the binary search condition k >= l+1), we have k-1 >= l. And k <= r from the range check. So both k-1 and k are in [l, r], and a[k-1] != a[k]. Output (k-1, k).
